three_body.instability_note: Moderate conflict between the three signals (29-pt spread). sector peer rank reads better than FTSE 100 position implies. The FTSE 100's overweight in energy and mining inflates relative ESG rankings.
three_body.resolution: Our resolution: Integrity 56/100 × WEM 34/100 → 'Engagement Priority'. Jangani, Date & Tucker (SSRN 5618192, 2026) show driver rankings shift across regime windows (COVID → COP26 → energy crisis). We anchor to verifiable harm metrics (emissions, pay ratios, enforcement records) that are regime-stable — not ESG labels that oscillate with market narrative.

summary_investor: BP plc is projected to hold steady in its energy peer ranking (3→3 of 5) over 3 months. FTSE 100 position: 21st→22nd percentile. WEM 34/100 — material ESG risk — recommend underweight or significant risk premium. 3 high-severity incidents (36mo) represent unpriced tail risk. Placebo Index 0.78 — ESG narrative overstates real performance.
summary_corporate: 12-month sector ranking forecast: rise (3→2 of 5). FTSE 100: 21st→24th percentile. Highest-leverage action: Physical Impacts of Climate Change (76% materiality) — this single driver has the greatest impact on FTSE 100 ranking improvement.
summary_auditor: Of 5 ESG claims analysed, 1 (20%) are independently verifiable. 1 claim(s) directly contradicted by external data — moderate restatement risk. ESG provider spread: 20 pts — significant methodological disagreement reflected in Integrity Score. Priority: third-party audit of Physical Impacts of Climate Change measurement methodology.
crowd_consensus: Aggregated signal across investor, corporate, and audit lenses: Lagging — underweight; high harm + ESG overstatement confirmed. Driver rankings are temporally unstable across market regimes (Jangani et al., SSRN 5618192). The 3-body instability — ESG score, peer rank, FTSE position pulling in different directions — is resolved by WEM (34/100) × Integrity (56/100): regime-stable signals grounded in verifiable harm, not rater methodology.
